(German pronunciation: [[ˈkʊpfɐtsɛl]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German")) is a small German town in the district of [Hohenlohe](/wiki/Hohenlohe_(district) "Hohenlohe (district)") in [Baden-Württemberg](/wiki/Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg "Baden-Württemberg"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") named after the Kupfer river flowing through it. The largest neighbouring towns are [Künzelsau](/wiki/K%C3%BCnzelsau "Künzelsau") (to the north) and [Schwäbisch Hall](/wiki/Schw%C3%A4bisch_Hall "Schwäbisch Hall") (to the south).

["Tabellengruppe 12411: Fortschreibung des Bevölkerungsstandes zum 31. Dezember 2024"](https://daten.statistik-bw.de/genesisonline/online?operation=statistic&levelindex=0&levelid=1757432774350&code=12411#abreadcrumb) [Update of the population as of 31 December 2024] (in German). [Statistisches Landesamt Baden-Württemberg](/wiki/Statistisches_Landesamt_Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg "Statistisches Landesamt Baden-Württemberg").

[Statistisches Bundesamt](/wiki/Statistisches_Bundesamt "Statistisches Bundesamt"), ed. (1983). *Historisches Gemeindeverzeichnis für die Bundesrepublik Deutschland. Namens-, Grenz- und Schlüsselnummernänderungen bei Gemeinden, Kreisen und Regierungsbezirken vom 27.5.1970 bis 31.12.1982*. Stuttgart/Mainz: W. Kohlhammer. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [3-17-003263-1](/wiki/Special:BookSources/3-17-003263-1 "Special:BookSources/3-17-003263-1").

**Kupferzell** (German pronunciation: [[ˈkʊpfɐtsɛl]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German")) is a small German town in the district of [Hohenlohe](/wiki/Hohenlohe_(district) "Hohenlohe (district)") in [Baden-Württemberg](/wiki/Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg "Baden-Württemberg"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") named after the Kupfer river flowing through it. The largest neighbouring towns are [Künzelsau](/wiki/K%C3%BCnzelsau "Künzelsau") (to the north) and [Schwäbisch Hall](/wiki/Schw%C3%A4bisch_Hall "Schwäbisch Hall") (to the south).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kupferzell&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Wackershofen_Freilandmuseum_Bahnhof_Kupferzell_2_20070530.jpg/250px-Wackershofen_Freilandmuseum_Bahnhof_Kupferzell_2_20070530.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wackershofen_Freilandmuseum_Bahnhof_Kupferzell_2_20070530.jpg)

Kupferzell old station, now at [Wackershofen](/w/index.php?title=Wackershofen&action=edit&redlink=1 "Wackershofen (page does not exist)") open air museum

Kupferzell was first mentioned in 1236 and as early as the 14th century, the Count of Hohenlohe owned property there.

During the late Middle Ages, Kupferzell always was referred to as "Celle", with a closer localization of "uf dem Ornwaldt". It was only in the 15th century that the current name appeared after its location on the [Kupfer (river)](/wiki/Kupfer_(river) "Kupfer (river)"). From 1721 to 1727/1729, Count Philipp Ernst had a castle built here. From 1767 until 1884 Kupferzell was the residence of the duke of [Hohenlohe-Waldenburg-Schillingsfürst](/wiki/Hohenlohe-Waldenburg-Schillingsf%C3%BCrst "Hohenlohe-Waldenburg-Schillingsfürst"); after mediatization in 1806, Kupferzell became part of the [Königreich Württemberg](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigreich_W%C3%BCrttemberg "Königreich Württemberg") under administration of the High Office of [Öhringen](/wiki/%C3%96hringen "Öhringen").[[3]](#cite_note-hohen-3)

On January 1, 1972, Kupferzell fused with Eschental, Feßbach, Mangoldsall, Goggenbach and Westernach to the town of Kupferzell.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

In March 2020, the town was particularly affected by [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") and became one of Germany's hotspots in the Hohenlohe district. Mid May, the [Robert Koch-Institut](/wiki/Robert_Koch-Institut "Robert Koch-Institut") started a seroepidemiology study named "Corona Monitoring lokal" attempting to enroll 2000 of the town's 6000 inhabitants for antibody testing and a throat swab for [SARS-CoV-2](/wiki/SARS-CoV-2 "SARS-CoV-2").[[5]](#cite_note-5)
